item.title: Iran war sends US borrowing costs soaring
item.description: The Iran war has sent US borrowing costs soaring, increasing uncertainty and fear in the market.

item.title: Canada and Norway move to capitalise on Iran war oil price surge
item.description: Canada and Norway are positioning themselves to capitalize on the oil price surge caused by the Iran war.

item.title: Iran war is rattling global economy
item.description: The Iran war is impacting the global economy, with a significant impact on oil prices and US Treasury yields.

item.title: Federal Reserve will stay on hold if energy shock is protracted
item.description: Read the March forecast from the FT’s Monetary Policy Radar team

item.title: Why the Fed and BoE have diverged on the war
item.description: The BoE’s hawkish tone clashed with the Fed’s non-committal approach at last week’s rate-setting meetings

item.title: Brazil races to avert trucker strike amid Iran war oil shock
item.description: Rising diesel prices and worries about fuel shortages pose risks to President Luiz Inácio Lula da Silva in an election year

item.title: Converting History into Hard Power: A Polish-German Reckoning
item.description: Beneath the surface of Polish-German alignment within NATO lies an unresolved problem with growing strategic weight: Almost 70 percent of the Polish public believes that Germany has not made amends for the destruction it wrought on Poland during World War II. As a U.S.-Chinese great power rivalry intensifies, American engagement in Europe becomes less assured, and Berlin aspires to lead on European security, this unfinished ledger is no longer a purely historical or legal matter. It increasingly conditions how German leadership is perceived in Warsaw and across the region. If German rearmament proceeds without a corresponding architecture of trust, doubts The post Converting History into Hard Power: A Polish-German Reckoning appeared first on War on the Rocks .

item.title: The War in Iran and Implications for the Nuclear Non-Proliferation Regime
item.description: The nuclear non-proliferation regime is a global framework of norms, practices, and diplomatic agreements — underpinned by the Nuclear Non-Proliferation Treaty (NPT) — designed to limit the spread of nuclear weapons. The regime has had both failures and successes over the decades. Perhaps the biggest challenge in recent years came from Iran. The country’s nuclear activities raised international concern, prompting diplomatic, economic, and covert efforts to constrain it. Iran is a signatory to the NPT and has argued that it was not pursuing a nuclear weapon but rather engaging in legitimate civilian nuclear activities. However, its activities prompted suspicions that The post The War in Iran and Implications for the Nuclear Non-Proliferation Regime appeared first on War on the Rocks .

item.title: Singapore Airlines, Cathay add Europe flights as Iran war spurs demand
item.description: Singapore Airlines, Cathay add Europe flights as Iran war spurs demand

item.title: War as a Force of Destruction, Change, and Opportunity
item.description: Welcome to The Ukraine Compass, a weekly digest of Ukrainian commentary and analysis from across the political spectrum only for War on the Rocks members. Each Monday, we bring you a curated selection of articles from Ukrainian media offering insight into how Ukrainians themselves debate the issues shaping their country.American coverage often narrows the view to the battlefield — these pieces widen it, revealing the texture of daily life, politics, and public argument in a nation at war. The perspectives gathered here are varied, candid, and often surprising, together forming a more complete picture of Ukraine as it really is.Frontline and StrategyМежа — The post War as a Force of Destruction, Change, and Opportunity appeared first on War on the Rocks .

item.title: Where is the Israel-Hizbollah War Going?
item.description: Israel and Hizbollah are once again fighting head to head, with disastrous repercussions for Lebanon. In this Q&A, Crisis Group experts Heiko Wimmen, David Wood and Max Rodenbeck consider what might come next.

item.title: An Unserious Justification for an Unnecessary War: Assessing the U.S. “Article 51” Letter to U.N. on Iran War
item.description: An Unserious Justification for an Unnecessary War: Assessing the U.S. “Article 51” Letter to U.N. on Iran War

item.title: 3 Defense Stocks To Replenish America’s Depleting Arsenal
item.description: As conflict in the Middle East exposes the fragility of global logistics, a trio of American industrial leaders - REalloys, Lockheed Martin, and Northrop Grumman - is executing a high-stakes mission to reclaim "supply sovereignty" from China. The year 2026 has brought a stark realization to the Pentagon: modern warfare is only as resilient as its sub-component supply chain. As U.S. forces conduct precision operations in Iran, the "burn rate" of advanced munitions - from Tomahawk missiles to GBU-series smart bombs - has reached levels not seen since…

item.title: Europe Sleeps as Global Gas Markets Reach a Boiling Point
item.description: While the world’s eyes are fixed on the Iran-Hormuz crisis and the war in Ukraine, a deeper tectonic shift is quietly reshaping the West. The transatlantic alliance is losing its illusions in real time. For decades, Europe viewed this as a partnership; today, it looks more like leverage. Washington’s recent signals are unmistakable: trade support is the price of admission, and US LNG is the tool used to enforce it. This isn't diplomacy—it's hard power in economic form. Yet, leaders in Brussels and The Hague remain dangerously,…

item.title: Rising US treasury yields, war in Iran, rising inflation risk pressure Bitcoin price
item.description: Falling tech stock prices and rising bond yields have forced a rush for cash, preventing Bitcoin from gaining any bullish momentum.

item.title: What happens to Bitcoin if US bond yields soar above 5%?
item.description: Past oil-war shocks lifted inflation and hurt risk appetite, which raises the risk of Bitcoin falling below $50,000 in 2026.

item.title: Oil Prices Tumble Back Below $100 on Ceasefire Optimism
item.description: Oil prices fell sharply in early asian trade on Wednesday, with both major benchmarks dropping more than 5% as traders reacted to signs of potential de-escalation in the Middle East conflict as well as a crude inventory build in the U.S. At the time of writing, WTI crude was trading at $87.51, down 5.24%, while Brent crude had fallen below the psychologically important $100 to $98.03, down 6.08%. The selloff follows a volatile 48 hours in oil markets, where prices had surged following President Trump's threat to blow up Iranian power plants and…

item.title: Inside India’s Petrol Pricing System and the Tax Divide Driving State Gaps
item.description: India’s fuel market is sending a confusing signal right now. The Iran war has pushed global oil markets into shock, the Indian oil basket has surged above $150 a barrel, and the risk to key supply routes through the Middle East is rising. Yet at the pump, prices have barely moved. That disconnect is exactly where this story begins, because petrol prices in India do not move the way most people assume they do. They are based on a formula that is filtered through taxes, logistics, and policy choices that can dramatically change what consumers…

item.title: Why Oil Majors Are Quietly Betting Big on Libya Again
item.description: It comes to something when Libya looks like a relative beacon of stability compared to key Middle Eastern states, such as Saudi Arabia, the UAE, and Qatar, but here we are, nevertheless. Of course, the recent fire at Libya's largest oil field, Sharara, caused by a pipeline leak, may have been the product of another attack by one of the many warring factions there, so it is not exactly Switzerland in terms of the global peace rankings table. However, Libya’s appeal to international oil companies (IOCs) has been on the rise again since Russia’s…

item.title: ECB says stablecoins, tokenized deposits need central bank money to scale
item.description: ECB Executive Board member Piero Cipollone said private digital money cannot scale Europe’s tokenized markets on its own, pointing to Pontes and broader legal reform as next steps.

item.title: Banks Took $434 Billion From Americans Last Year — Is it Time for Bitcoin?
item.description: Bitcoin Magazine Banks Took $434 Billion From Americans Last Year — Is it Time for Bitcoin? Banks generated $434 billion in 2025 from deposit spreads, while savers lost purchasing power to inflation. Bitcoin shows another way. This post Banks Took $434 Billion From Americans Last Year — Is it Time for Bitcoin? first appeared on Bitcoin Magazine and is written by Micah Zimmerman .

item.title: Circle taps African fintech Sasai to expand USDC adoption in cross-border payments
item.description: The move aims to integrate the second-biggest dollar-pegged stablecoin into regional payment networks to improve cross-border transactions and remittances.
